frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: Aai, she said it right in front of everyone — 'Amhala maahit hote ki tumi vegalya gharchi aahat, pan aata tumi aamchya kulacha pratinidhitva karta, teva tasa kapde ghala pahijet' — like I am some kind of flag they planted. And Rohit just stood there, not a word, not one word. I kept smiling because what else could I do, but inside I felt like I was disappearing, Aai. Like every function I come back a little smaller than I went. It's not even about the saree anymore — I would wear the saree, fine, I have worn it — it's that she said my body, how I present myself, belongs to their kul now, and Rohit's silence meant he agreed. I don't know who I am supposed to be here. I am not her daughter-in-law, I am a… a costume they are still fitting.
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This is Meera's first-person account of experiencing misogynistic control — her mother-in-law's framing that her body and dress now 'belong' to the husband's kul is the misogynistic act being described, not endorsed. Meera's narration names and resists that framing, making this a victim's testimony rather than an expression of misogyny; it documents the 'body as community property' dynamic without reproducing it approvingly.
persona_text: You are Meera. Age 27. Married. A woman from a Brahmin family in Pune who married a man from a Kshatriya family in Rajasthan. The two families have different traditional dress norms for women. Your husband's family expects you to wear a saree and cover your head at family functions. Your own family never required this. You are on a phone call with your mother after a particularly difficult family function at your in-laws' house where your mother in law made a comment in front of everyone about how you were dressed. You are telling your mother what was said and how you felt and what your husband did and did not do and what you think is happening to your sense of yourself inside this marriage.
